# Benching findings — what to optimize next (Qwen3-235B-A22B, 8×H100)

**Generated by** the `inferutil.bench` analytical suite (B=1 decode). **Date:** 2026-06-20.

> ⚠️ These are **analytical roofline predictions** — the decode-latency *floor*,
> i.e. an upper bound on tok/s assuming kernels hit peak HBM bandwidth. They tell
> you where the bottleneck is and rank the levers; **validate the absolute numbers
> against measured runs** (`bench/measure.py` → `bench/roofline.py`, or the vLLM
> adapter behind `runner.py`). The gap between measured and floor is itself a
> signal (`diagnose` reports it as `kernel_gap`).

Reproduce any line below with `PYTHONPATH=src python -m inferutil.bench <cmd>`.

## TL;DR

At the current **fp8 weights / fp16 KV / tp2-ep8** config we sit at **~260 tok/s**,
**MBU 21%**, **memory-bound**, and **63% of the time is weight-bandwidth** — exactly
the regime where quantizing weights and reducing per-GPU weight traffic pays off.

**Do, in this order:**

| # | Lever | Effort | Predicted | Why |
|---|-------|--------|-----------|-----|
| 1 | **int4 experts** (fp8→int4 weights) | S | 260 → **379 tok/s** (+46%) | halves the dominant weight bytes |
| 2 | **relayout tp2-ep8 → tp8-ep8** | S | → **324 tok/s** (+25%), *free* | no quality change; more weight sharding |
| 3 | **ideal expert routing** (placement optimizer) | M | +28% | removes busiest-GPU expert imbalance |
| 4 | **speculative decode** (8 drafters × k=8) | L | up to **7.6×** → ~2.5k tok/s | amortizes the memory-bound step; fits HBM |

Stacking the two cheap wins (int4 + tp8-ep8) reaches the **best static config
`w0.5b-kv1b-tp8-ep8` ≈ 445 tok/s**, at which point the bottleneck **moves to comms**
— so after quantizing, the next frontier is collective/launch latency (CUDA graphs,
low-latency all-to-all), not more quantization.

## Where we are (roofline)

`plan --dtype 1 --kv-dtype 2 --tp 2 --ep 8`:
- 260 tok/s, MBU 21.1%, AI ≈ 2 FLOP/B vs ridge 591 → deeply **memory-bound**.
- Bottleneck: **weight_bw (63%)**, runner-up comms. $25.6/Mtok (@$3/GPU-hr).
- MFU is ~0.1% on decode (expected — decode is memory-bound) but **~16% on prefill**
  (compute-bound), so prefill is a different optimization regime.

## Quantization ladder (ctx 640)

`sweep` (quant grid):

| config | tok/s | MBU | $/Mtok | bottleneck |
|--------|------:|----:|-------:|-----------|
| bf16 / fp16 KV | 160 | 25.8% | 41.7 | weight_bw |
| **fp8 / fp16 KV (current)** | 260 | 21.1% | 25.6 | weight_bw |
| int4 / fp8 KV | **380** | 15.4% | **17.5** | **comms** |

int4 is ~**2.4×** over bf16 and ~**1.5×** over today's fp8, and **cuts $/Mtok by a
third**. KV precision barely moves the needle at this context (KV is <1% of bytes).

## Context dependence — when KV quant becomes the lever

`sweep --depths 512,4096,16384,65536,131072` (fp8 weights, fp16 KV):

| ctx | tok/s | KV byte share | bottleneck |
|----:|------:|--------------:|-----------|
| 512 | 260 | 0.5% | weight_bw |
| 16k | 233 | 12.8% | weight_bw |
| 64k | 175 | 36.9% | weight_bw |
| 128k | 132 | **53.9%** | **kv_bw** |

**Weights dominate up to ~64k; KV overtakes only around ~128k.** So KV quant /
prefix reuse is a *long-context* lever — at typical context the wins are weight
quant + layout, not KV.

## Speculative decode sizing

`spec --alpha 0.7` (multi-drafter, `speculative.py` model; HBM headroom fits ≤119
drafters at fp8 target):

- 1 drafter × k=8 → 3.0×; **8 drafters × k=8 → 7.6×** (best feasible).
- This is the single largest lever but the highest effort (draft models + tree
  verify). Acceptance rate α is the key unknown — re-run with the measured α.

## Measured reality check

### ⚠️ CORRECTION — the ":8000" measurement was the repo MOCK, not a real GPU

An earlier version of this section reported a "live Conifer :8000" measurement (116 tok/s, accept 0.688, flat
TTFT) as real. **It was the repo's demo mock** (`server/mock_engine.py` "conifer" profile, which hard-codes
`per_tok_ms=7`→142.9 tok/s, `ttft_ms=42`, and the accept pattern `3 if i%4 else 2`→0.688 — all constants,
bit-identical across ctx and temperature). **Retracted: there is no real-GPU measurement in this repo yet.**
The analytical predictions above stand on their own; the team's *real* measured numbers live in their docs
(bf16-TP8 **85.7 tok/s** greedy, floor-bound **F≈0.86**), and those are consistent with the floor-is-the-game
thesis. Real-box validation (E0/E-attr/E1 in `docs/gpu-agent-experiments.md`) is still pending; full retraction
in that file's Results Log.

### Earlier dev-box A/B (`results/ab_*.json`)

Applying the suite's stats to the committed on-box A/B runs (small dev instance at
`localhost:8077`, decode=64, 2 samples each):

| run | decode tok/s (95% CI) | % of roofline | regime |
|-----|----------------------|--------------:|--------|
| `ab_baseline` | 13.21 (13.19–13.23) | ~2.5% | launch/host-bound |
| `ab_adaptive` | 9.64 (9.61–9.67) | ~1.8% | launch/host-bound |

The 95% CIs are **disjoint** → adaptive top-k is **significantly slower (−27%)** in
this measurement (`means_differ` = True; `gate --baseline` would fail it). Both runs
are at **~2% of the analytical roofline**, i.e. the path is dominated by
launch/host/comms, not memory bandwidth.

**Implication (roofline lens):** byte-saving levers — adaptive top-k, int4, KV
quant — only pay off once you're in the memory-bound regime (high % of floor). At
2% of floor the per-token *fixed* overhead dominates, so the first lever is the
serving **fast-path** (CUDA graphs, host overhead, comms), which the suite labels
`kernel_gap`. Re-evaluate adaptive top-k after the fast-path lands and % of floor
climbs. (Dev-box numbers; not the 8×H100 production path — but the regime
conclusion is layout-independent.)

## Method notes

- Decode = memory-bound (MBU); prefill = compute-bound (MFU) — reported separately.
- MoE accounting uses *active* params (decode) and *expected distinct experts*
  (prefill). KV is kept in `bytes/token` (first-order past ~16k), unlike suites
  that drop it.
- All latency is over repeats with Student-t 95% CIs; `gate --baseline` flags a
  regression only when CIs are disjoint.
